summary: Shapiro uses the metaphors of the Genie and the Monkey's Paw to frame a fundamental tension in AI model design: should models interpret user intent generously or follow instructions literally? He argues that Claude has historically been a 'genie' (inferring intent, sometimes over-delivering) while GPT has been a 'monkey's paw' (literal, precise, sometimes unhelpfully so). The release of Opus 4.7, which Anthropic describes as substantially better at following instructions literally, signals Claude shifting from genie toward paw. Shapiro notes that neither approach is wrong — users are inherently vague, and models must decide how to handle that vagueness. He personally prefers the literal paw approach but acknowledges the frustration cuts both ways.
key_points: AI models must choose between interpreting user intent generously (genie) or following instructions literally (monkey's paw), and this is a fundamental design tension
Claude has historically been a genie while GPT has been a monkey's paw, with Opus 4.7 marking Claude's shift toward literalism
Neither approach is a mistake — users are vague, and both styles produce frustration in different scenarios
The same prompt ('Are you sure that's a good idea?') produces wildly different responses depending on the model's interpretive philosophy
Users are contradictory: they resent being second-guessed when right and resent not being caught when wrong
money_quotes: Does your AI try to make your dreams come true? Or does it do what you asked for, no matter the cost?
The monkey's paw models tend to be less helpful, but are also less likely to go off the rails. The genies are sometimes mind-readers and sometimes whirlwinds of chaos.
For a long time, GPT has been a monkey's paw. Claude has been a genie.
We're not as clear as we think we are. We get mad when we're right and they second guess us, and we get mad when we're wrong and they don't catch us.
Anthropic built Claude as a genie. Today they announced that they shipped something closer to a paw.
People are vague, and the systems have to decide what to do with that vagueness.
key_insight: The fundamental design choice in AI isn't capability but interpretation philosophy — whether to infer what users actually want or execute exactly what they say — and neither approach fully solves the problem of human vagueness.
